danger_signals.visual_form: Distinct character sprite designs with faction-specific clothing (greasers, mohawked punkers, schoolgirl gang members)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate combat threat; player must engage or avoid

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger, more detailed sprites with unique visual styling (Jack's headband, Joel's mohawk, Kim's cheongsam-style dress, Sabu's green outfit)
danger_signals.meaning: Major difficulty escalation; stage-ending confrontation required

danger_signals.visual_form: Multiple enemy sprites occupying stage area simultaneously
danger_signals.meaning: High combat pressure; player surrounded or outnumbered

danger_signals.visual_form: Underground subway setting (US version), shabby architecture, deteriorated walls visible in backgrounds
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile territory; lawless zone where violence is normalized

safety_signals.visual_form: Hamburger sprite (US version) or rice ball sprite (JP version)
safety_signals.meaning: Player health restoration available; brief vulnerability window resolved

safety_signals.visual_form: Boss defeat triggers transition; in Japanese version, Hiroshi reunion scene plays (showing friend safety)
Empty stage areas without enemy sprites

faction_indicators.color_identity: Yellow/tan leather jackets (visual variance from JP schoolboy delinquents)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Greaser aesthetic with slicked hair; gruff appearance
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard greaser punks vs. stronger variants
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Underground subway station (US version); Shinjuku Station (JP version)

faction_indicators.color_identity: School uniform variants; blue school uniforms in JP version
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: School gang insignia implied through uniform styling
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Rank-and-file delinquents vs. boss Riki/Jack (features headband, open vest, tape around abdomen)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Daytime/sunset/nighttime outdoor areas (difficulty-dependent in JP Famicom; stage 2 retains this effect in NES)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Dark punker fashion with mohawk hairstyles and face coverings
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mohawk and punk aesthetic signals rebellion and aggression
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Generic punkers vs. boss Shinji/Joel (distinctive mohawk + ponytail combination)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Bar setting with beer advertisement signs (visible in both versions despite censorship controversy)

faction_indicators.color_identity: Red dresses (US redrawn version); original blue school uniforms (JP version)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Schoolgirl aesthetic; sukeban (female gang leader) designation in JP
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Standard schoolgirl gang members vs. boss Misuzu/Kim (cheongsam-style red dress, more imposing presence)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Stage environment unspecified in sources; part of progression toward final confrontation

faction_indicators.color_identity: Green outfit (JP and US versions; potential organized crime signification)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Sabu identified as yakuza boss; highest authority antagonist
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss only; no weaker variants documented
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Final confrontation space; location unspecified in sources

faction_indicators.color_identity: Brown vest and brown pants (US version); white Japanese school uniform (JP version); skin tone pink (US) vs. yellow (JP)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Protagonist hero; Kunio (JP) or Mr. K/Renegade (US)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Visually distinct from all enemy factions through costume choice

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Skyline color changes in Stages 1-2: blue (daytime/Level 1 easy), orange (sunset/Level 2 medium), black (nighttime/Level 3 hard)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy AI aggression and health scaling correlates with environmental darkness; more difficult encounters in night settings

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Opening sequence shows Hiroshi abduction by car; closing sequence shows Kunio and Hiroshi reunion with handshake and crying emotion
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Story framing motivates player progression; emotional payoff signals successful completion

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Each stage environment visually transformed by gang presence: underground subway (greaser gang), outdoor stations (delinquent gang), bar area (punker gang), school/final areas (schoolgirl and yakuza gangs)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must defeat stage bosses to progress through occupied territories; each faction behaves as territorial controllers

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Urban decay and lawlessness (US localization emphasis)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Title screen shows deteriorated wall with spray-painted 'RENEGADE' text; shabby underground subway aesthetics; beer advertisements visible despite censorship concerns
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Normalizes street violence and gang activity as environmental baseline; no authority presence documented

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: JP version: high school setting with school uniforms and student-gang themes. US version: street criminal aesthetic inspired by The Warriors film; character redesigns toward Western greaser/punk subculture
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Same gameplay mechanics repurposed with different thematic framing; protagonist motivation shifts from helping friend to rescuing girlfriend from mob

environmental_storytelling.example: Stage 1 location shifts from Shinjuku Station (JP, outdoor, culturally grounded) to underground subway (US, urban decay emphasis). Visual change communicates location control by greaser gang and world-state shift toward criminalized urban space.

Kunio's school uniform (JP) vs. Mr. K's brown vest (US) signals cultural narrative difference: student defending classmate honor vs. street brawler rescuing kidnapped girlfriend. Identical gameplay systems but opposite world-state implications.
Beer advertisement signs in bar setting (Stage 3) signal adult criminal environment despite Nintendo censorship concerns. Detail communicates gritty urban setting to player.

Each stage boss features unique, more detailed sprite work and distinctive accessories (headbands, mohawks, tattape, cheongsams) compared to generic gang members. Visual hierarchy communicates authority structure and danger escalation.
Sabu yakuza boss wears green outfit (both versions); distinct from other faction color palettes. Color isolation signals final antagonist and highest criminal authority.
Multiple enemy sprites per stage communicate gang size and territorial control. Denser crowds in later stages signal escalating opposition to player's rescue mission progression.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Spawn and move toward player; initiate combat; require multiple hits to stun before collar grab available
Requires sustained combo attacks; cannot be collar-grabbed or sat-on until health depleted significantly; stage-ending encounter
Cluster behavior; multiple simultaneous combatants; higher health/endurance than Stage 1 enemies
Mohawk visual signals distinctive threat; cannot be sat-on or collar-grabbed until critically wounded; forces extended combat sequences
Sukeban/gang leader authority; requires damage threshold before grapple moves available; stage-progression gatekeeper
Unique exception: cannot be sat-on at any health threshold (per sources); requires pure combat pressure; represents highest difficulty and organizational authority
Health pool depletes through enemy contact; health recovery via hamburger/rice ball item; limited lives with no continues; must navigate multi-stage gauntlet
Appears abducted in opening sequence; reappears crying during ending reunion sequence; motivates narrative progression

notes: Renegade demonstrates significant localization-driven world-state shifts. The Japanese version (Nekketsu Kōha Kunio-kun) frames world-state through high school honor culture and student gang conflicts, while US localization recontextualizes identical gameplay as street criminal underworld rescue narrative inspired by The Warriors film. Visual manifestation differs substantially (school uniforms vs. street fashion, outdoor stations vs. urban decay subway), but danger signals, faction identifiers, and mechanical progression remain structurally identical. Difficulty-dependent environmental coloring (daytime/sunset/night skylines) in JP Famicom communicates world-state progression through visual tone rather than explicit mechanics. Boss characters' resistance to certain grapple moves (inability to sit-on until critically wounded, with Sabu as exception) creates behavioral tells of authority hierarchy. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on character costume design and stage setting transformation rather than narrative exposition.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Renegade NES article)
